g.text: Total: 64 bytes (fits in one L1 cache line)
Single shift+AND — no branch, no loop
WHY You Cannot Skip Unmatched Syscalls at ptrace Level
Timing: Recorded for ALL Syscalls, Filter Applied After
nr | call_count | u64 | incremented every exit
nr | error_count | u64 | incremented if retval in [-4095,-1]
nr | total_ns | u64 | += elapsed_ns every exit
Sorted by total_ns desc at session end for -c output
Reason 1: Entry/Exit Toggle Must Stay Synced
Reason 2: ptrace Has No Per-Syscall Intercept
Correct Order of Operations at EXIT Stop
5 | format line into outbuf | matched only
Stats table counts ALL syscalls regardless of filter
Every Syscall — Two Stops — No Exceptions
ENTRY stop | tracee executes syscall insn | orig_rax=nr, rdi-r9=args
kernel executes syscall | (tracer resumes with PTRACE_SYSCALL)
EXIT stop | kernel returns to userspace | rax=retval, orig_rax preserved
TIF_SYSCALL_TRACE set for ALL syscalls | cannot be selective per-nr
EXIT stop: snprintf(') = retval\n') → outbuf + len
EXIT stop: fputs(outbuf, g_output) ← single write(), atomic
Atomic write prevents interleaving across concurrent PIDs
per-PID toggle — MUST flip on EVERY stop
stop N | in_syscall=0 | ENTRY — read orig_rax
stop N+1 | in_syscall=1 | EXIT — read rax
skip mmap entry → toggle=1 | next stop=mmap EXIT | reads as ENTRY → WRONG nr
one missed stop corrupts all subsequent syscalls forever

text.tspan: PTRACE_SYSCALL = stop on ALL syscall boundaries.
No kernel API to say 'only stop on open/read/write'.
Only seccomp-BPF can filter at the kernel level.
